definition: The aging farmer problem manifests not as output decline (TFP grows 1.4%/year) but as reduced adaptive capacity to shocks. Capital-intensive row crops are technology-driven and age-insensitive, but adaptive capacity (crop rotation, climate shock response, variety adoption) degrades with farmer age. This reframes the issue from slow production decline to fat-tail amplification.
evidence: US median farmer age 58, only 9% under 35
States with younger farmer demographics recovered yields 15-20% faster after 2012 drought
open_questions: Can precision agriculture technology compensate for reduced adaptive capacity?
Is the 2012 drought recovery data generalizable to other shock types?
How does farm consolidation interact with the aging farmer effect?
